title: Writing - Part 1: Youth Unemployment
description: Your class has been discussing the causes of youth unemployment and how it could be reduced.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the causes in your notes. You should explain which cause you consider most important, giving reasons for your opinion.

causes: Mismatch between education and job market
opinions: Many young people struggle to find jobs because employers expect experience they have not yet had the chance to gain.
Universities often prepare students for academic careers rather than the practical skills businesses need.
In times of economic downturn, young people are among the first to lose jobs or fail to find new ones.

structure.introduction: Introduce youth unemployment as a serious economic and social challenge.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first cause and its effect on young jobseekers.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second cause and argue which is more fundamental.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your analysis and suggest how the problem might be addressed.

content.modelAnswer:
Youth unemployment remains one of the most persistent challenges in many countries, with serious consequences for individuals and societies alike. Two important causes are the lack of practical work experience and the mismatch between educational qualifications and labour market needs.

Many young graduates face a frustrating paradox: they cannot get a job without experience, but they cannot gain experience without a job. Internship programmes and apprenticeships can help bridge this gap, but access to quality opportunities is not equal for all, leaving many young people disadvantaged from the start.

Equally significant is the disconnect between what universities teach and what employers require. Degrees in certain fields produce graduates with theoretical knowledge but few of the practical, digital or interpersonal skills that modern workplaces demand. Reforming curricula to include more work-based learning could substantially improve young people's employability.

In conclusion, while both causes are serious, I believe the education-skills mismatch is the more fundamental issue. Aligning academic programmes with the needs of a changing economy would help a whole generation enter the workforce with confidence.
